summary: Fowler identifies a dysfunctional architectural pattern where organizations mandate the use of expensive infrastructure software regardless of project fit. The pattern stems from the sunk cost fallacy: having already paid for something, leadership insists on using it to avoid feeling the investment was wasted. This results in projects forced to adopt unsuitable tools, creating extra effort and suboptimal outcomes. The brief post satirically names this anti-pattern to make it recognizable and discussable.
key_points: Organizations often mandate use of expensive software purchases regardless of project suitability
The sunk cost fallacy drives architectural decisions when it shouldn't
Forcing unsuitable infrastructure onto projects creates unnecessary extra effort
Past spending on tools should not dictate future technical choices
money_quotes: I find this to be a sadly common architectural style.
Your company buys some very expensive piece of infrastructure software.
You are then told you must use it on a project even if it's not suitable for the project and causes you extra effort.
After paying all that money for it you don't want it to go to waste do you?
key_insight: Past investment in a technology should never justify forcing its use on projects where it doesn't fit—that's the sunk cost fallacy masquerading as architecture.
